question:
Problem A-5
Let \( a, b, c \), and \( d \) be positive integers and
\[
r=1-\frac{a}{b}-\frac{c}{d}
\]

Given that \( a+c \leqslant 1982 \) and \( r>0 \), prove that
\[
r>\frac{1}{1983^{3}} .
\]
solution:
A-5.
We are given that
\[
r=1-\frac{a}{b}-\frac{c}{d}=\frac{b d-a d-b c}{b d}>0 .
\]

Thus \( b d-a d-b c \) is a positive integer and so \( r \geqslant 1 / b d \). We may assume without loss of generality that \( b \leqslant d \). If \( b \leqslant d \leqslant 1983, r \geqslant 1983^{-2}>1983^{-3} \). Since \( a+c \leqslant 1982 \), if \( 1983 \leqslant b \leqslant \) \( d \), one has
\[
r \geqslant 1-\frac{a}{1983}-\frac{c}{1983} \geqslant 1-\frac{1982}{1983}=\frac{1}{1983}>\frac{1}{1983^{3}} .
\]

The remaining case is that with \( b<1983<d \). Then the \( d \) that minimizes \( r \) for fixed \( a, b, c \) is \( 1+[b c /(b-a)] \), where \( [x] \) is the greatest integer in \( x \). This \( d \) is at most \( 1983 b \) since \( b-a \geqslant 1 \) and \( c<1982 \) and thus
\[
r \geqslant \frac{1}{b d} \geqslant \frac{1}{1983 b^{2}}>\frac{1}{1983^{3}} .
\]

Hence we have the desired inequality in all cases.

descriptive_long.question:
Problem A-5
Let \( integera, integerb, integerc \), and \( integerd \) be positive integers and
\[
residual=1-\frac{integera}{integerb}-\frac{integerc}{integerd}
\]

Given that \( integera+integerc \leqslant 1982 \) and \( residual>0 \), prove that
\[
residual>\frac{1}{1983^{3}} .
\]
descriptive_long.solution:
A-5.
We are given that
\[
residual=1-\frac{integera}{integerb}-\frac{integerc}{integerd}=\frac{integerb integerd-integera integerd-integerb integerc}{integerb integerd}>0 .
\]

Thus \( integerb integerd-integera integerd-integerb integerc \) is a positive integer and so \( residual \geqslant 1 / integerb integerd \). We may assume without loss of generality that \( integerb \leqslant integerd \). If \( integerb \leqslant integerd \leqslant 1983, residual \geqslant 1983^{-2}>1983^{-3} \). Since \( integera+integerc \leqslant 1982 \), if \( 1983 \leqslant integerb \leqslant \) \( integerd \), one has
\[
residual \geqslant 1-\frac{integera}{1983}-\frac{integerc}{1983} \geqslant 1-\frac{1982}{1983}=\frac{1}{1983}>\frac{1}{1983^{3}} .
\]

The remaining case is that with \( integerb<1983<integerd \). Then the \( integerd \) that minimizes \( residual \) for fixed \( integera, integerb, integerc \) is \( 1+[integerb integerc /(integerb-integera)] \), where \( [x] \) is the greatest integer in \( x \). This \( integerd \) is at most \( 1983 integerb \) since \( integerb-integera \geqslant 1 \) and \( integerc<1982 \) and thus
\[
residual \geqslant \frac{1}{integerb integerd} \geqslant \frac{1}{1983 integerb^{2}}>\frac{1}{1983^{3}} .
\]

Hence we have the desired inequality in all cases.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Problem A-5
Let \( pineapple, honeycomb, butterfly \), and \( framework \) be positive integers and
\[
lighthouse = 1-\frac{pineapple}{honeycomb}-\frac{butterfly}{framework}
\]

Given that \( pineapple + butterfly \leqslant 1982 \) and \( lighthouse > 0 \), prove that
\[
lighthouse > \frac{1}{1983^{3}} .
\]
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
A-5.
We are given that
\[
lighthouse = 1-\frac{pineapple}{honeycomb}-\frac{butterfly}{framework}=\frac{honeycomb framework-pineapple framework-honeycomb butterfly}{honeycomb framework}>0 .
\]

Thus \( honeycomb framework-pineapple framework-honeycomb butterfly \) is a positive integer and so \( lighthouse \geqslant 1 / honeycomb framework \). We may assume without loss of generality that \( honeycomb \leqslant framework \). If \( honeycomb \leqslant framework \leqslant 1983, lighthouse \geqslant 1983^{-2}>1983^{-3} \). Since \( pineapple + butterfly \leqslant 1982 \), if \( 1983 \leqslant honeycomb \leqslant \) \( framework \), one has
\[
lighthouse \geqslant 1-\frac{pineapple}{1983}-\frac{butterfly}{1983} \geqslant 1-\frac{1982}{1983}=\frac{1}{1983}>\frac{1}{1983^{3}} .
\]

The remaining case is that with \( honeycomb<1983<framework \). Then the \( framework \) that minimizes \( lighthouse \) for fixed \( pineapple, honeycomb, butterfly \) is \( 1+[ honeycomb butterfly /( honeycomb-pineapple ) ] \), where \( [x] \) is the greatest integer in \( x \). This \( framework \) is at most \( 1983 honeycomb \) since \( honeycomb-pineapple \geqslant 1 \) and \( butterfly<1982 \) and thus
\[
lighthouse \geqslant \frac{1}{honeycomb framework} \geqslant \frac{1}{1983 honeycomb^{2}}>\frac{1}{1983^{3}} .
\]

Hence we have the desired inequality in all cases.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Problem A-5
Let \( noninteger, irrational, negativeval \), and \( zeroelement \) be positive integers and
\[
completeness = 1-\frac{noninteger}{irrational}-\frac{negativeval}{zeroelement}
\]

Given that \( noninteger + negativeval \leqslant 1982 \) and \( completeness > 0 \), prove that
\[
completeness > \frac{1}{1983^{3}} .
\]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
A-5.
We are given that
\[
completeness = 1-\frac{noninteger}{irrational}-\frac{negativeval}{zeroelement}=\frac{irrational zeroelement-noninteger zeroelement-irrational negativeval}{irrational zeroelement}>0 .
\]

Thus \( irrational zeroelement-noninteger zeroelement-irrational negativeval \) is a positive integer and so \( completeness \geqslant 1 / irrational zeroelement \). We may assume without loss of generality that \( irrational \leqslant zeroelement \). If \( irrational \leqslant zeroelement \leqslant 1983, completeness \geqslant 1983^{-2}>1983^{-3} \). Since \( noninteger + negativeval \leqslant 1982 \), if \( 1983 \leqslant irrational \leqslant \) \( zeroelement \), one has
\[
completeness \geqslant 1-\frac{noninteger}{1983}-\frac{negativeval}{1983} \geqslant 1-\frac{1982}{1983}=\frac{1}{1983}>\frac{1}{1983^{3}} .
\]

The remaining case is that with \( irrational < 1983 < zeroelement \). Then the \( zeroelement \) that minimizes \( completeness \) for fixed \( noninteger, irrational, negativeval \) is \( 1+[irrational negativeval /(irrational - noninteger)] \), where \( [x] \) is the greatest integer in \( x \). This \( zeroelement \) is at most \( 1983 irrational \) since \( irrational - noninteger \geqslant 1 \) and \( negativeval < 1982 \) and thus
\[
completeness \geqslant \frac{1}{irrational zeroelement} \geqslant \frac{1}{1983 irrational^{2}} > \frac{1}{1983^{3}} .
\]

Hence we have the desired inequality in all cases.

garbled_string.question:
Problem A-5
Let \( qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla, mbplxqre \), and \( vgdhcani \) be positive integers and
\[
sxnfolew=1-\frac{qzxwvtnp}{hjgrksla}-\frac{mbplxqre}{vgdhcani}
\]

Given that \( qzxwvtnp+mbplxqre \leqslant 1982 \) and \( sxnfolew>0 \), prove that
\[
sxnfolew>\frac{1}{1983^{3}} .
\]
garbled_string.solution:
A-5.
We are given that
\[
sxnfolew=1-\frac{qzxwvtnp}{hjgrksla}-\frac{mbplxqre}{vgdhcani}=\frac{hjgrksla vgdhcani-qzxwvtnp vgdhcani-hjgrksla mbplxqre}{hjgrksla vgdhcani}>0 .
\]

Thus \( hjgrksla vgdhcani-qzxwvtnp vgdhcani-hjgrksla mbplxqre \) is a positive integer and so \( sxnfolew \geqslant 1 / hjgrksla vgdhcani \). We may assume without loss of generality that \( hjgrksla \leqslant vgdhcani \). If \( hjgrksla \leqslant vgdhcani \leqslant 1983, sxnfolew \geqslant 1983^{-2}>1983^{-3} \). Since \( qzxwvtnp+mbplxqre \leqslant 1982 \), if \( 1983 \leqslant hjgrksla \leqslant vgdhcani \), one has
\[
sxnfolew \geqslant 1-\frac{qzxwvtnp}{1983}-\frac{mbplxqre}{1983} \geqslant 1-\frac{1982}{1983}=\frac{1}{1983}>\frac{1}{1983^{3}} .
\]

The remaining case is that with \( hjgrksla<1983<vgdhcani \). Then the \( vgdhcani \) that minimizes \( sxnfolew \) for fixed \( qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla, mbplxqre \) is \( 1+[hjgrksla mbplxqre /(hjgrksla-qzxwvtnp)] \), where \( [x] \) is the greatest integer in \( x \). This \( vgdhcani \) is at most \( 1983 hjgrksla \) since \( hjgrksla-qzxwvtnp \geqslant 1 \) and \( mbplxqre<1982 \) and thus
\[
sxnfolew \geqslant \frac{1}{hjgrksla vgdhcani} \geqslant \frac{1}{1983 hjgrksla^{2}}>\frac{1}{1983^{3}} .
\]

Hence we have the desired inequality in all cases.

kernel_variant.question:
Let  
\[
a_{1},a_{2},a_{3},a_{4},\;b_{1},b_{2},b_{3},b_{4}\in\mathbb Z_{>0}
\]  
satisfy  
\[
\gcd(a_{i},b_{i})=1\quad(i=1,2,3,4),\qquad 
2\le b_{1}\le b_{2}\le b_{3}\le b_{4}\le 2021 ,
\]  
together with the divisibility chain  
\[
b_{2}\mid b_{3}\mid b_{4}.
\]  
Put  
\[
S:=a_{1}+a_{2}+a_{3}+a_{4}\le 2020,\qquad  
r:=1-\frac{a_{1}}{b_{1}}-\frac{a_{2}}{b_{2}}
        -\frac{a_{3}}{b_{3}}-\frac{a_{4}}{b_{4}}>0 .
\]

(a)\;  Prove the universal estimate  
\[
\boxed{\, r\ge\dfrac{1}{2019\times 2021}\,}
\]  
and prove that the denominator $2019\times 2021=4\,080\,399$ cannot be
replaced by any \emph{smaller} positive integer that depends only on the
upper bound $2021$ for the $b_{i}$.

(b)\;  Describe \emph{all} $8$-tuples  
\[
(a_{1},a_{2},a_{3},a_{4};\,b_{1},b_{2},b_{3},b_{4})
\]  
for which  
\[
\boxed{\, r=\dfrac{1}{b_{1} b_{4}}\,}
\]  
holds.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout write  
\[
L:=\operatorname{lcm}(b_{1},b_{2},b_{3},b_{4}),\qquad 
r=\frac{k}{L}\quad(k\in\mathbb Z_{>0}).
\]

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%  
\textbf{1.\;Part (a) - the best possible lower bound.}

Because  
\[
k=L-\sum_{i=1}^{4}a_{i}\,\frac{L}{b_{i}}\ge 1 ,
\]
one has  
\[
r=\frac{k}{L}\ge\frac{1}{L}.                                     \tag{1.1}
\]
The divisibility chain implies  
\[
L=\operatorname{lcm}(b_{1},b_{2},b_{3},b_{4})
  =\operatorname{lcm}(b_{1},b_{4})\le b_{1}b_{4},                 \tag{1.2}
\]
hence  
\[
r\ge\frac{1}{b_{1}b_{4}}.                                         \tag{1.3}
\]

If $b_{1}\le 2019$ then $r\ge 1/(b_{1}b_{4})\ge 1/(2019\cdot 2021)$
because $b_{4}\le 2021$.  It remains to treat $b_{1}=2020$ and
$b_{1}=2021$.

\emph{Case $b_{1}=2021$.}\;  Then $b_{i}\ge 2021$, so  
\[
r=1-\sum_{i=1}^{4}\frac{a_{i}}{b_{i}}
     \ge 1-\frac{S}{2021}
     \ge 1-\frac{2020}{2021}= \frac{1}{2021}
     >\frac{1}{2019\cdot 2021}.
\]

\emph{Case $b_{1}=2020$.}  
Two sub-cases occur.

(i)\; $b_{2}=b_{3}=b_{4}=2020$.  Then  
$r=1-S/2020\ge 1/2020>1/(2019\cdot 2021)$.

(ii)\;  At least one of $b_{2},b_{3},b_{4}$ equals $2021$.  The
divisibility relations force $b_{2}=b_{3}=b_{4}=2021$, and writing
$T:=a_{2}+a_{3}+a_{4}$ one gets $T\ge 3$ and $a_{1}=2020-T$.  Hence  
\[
r
=1-\frac{a_{1}}{2020}-\frac{T}{2021}
=1-\frac{2020-T}{2020}-\frac{T}{2021}
=\frac{T}{2020}-\frac{T}{2021}
     =\frac{T}{2020\cdot 2021}
     \ge\frac{3}{2020\cdot 2021}
     >\frac{1}{2019\cdot 2021 }.
\]

Combining all cases proves  
\[
\boxed{\,r\ge\frac{1}{2019\cdot 2021}\,}.
\]

\emph{Optimality.}\;  The tuple  
\[
(1009,1,1,1009;\,2019,2021,2021,2021)
\]
satisfies all hypotheses and gives $r=1/(2019\cdot 2021)$, so the
constant $2019\cdot 2021$ is the smallest possible. \hfill$\square$

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%  
\textbf{2.\;Preparations for part (b).}

Assume from now on that  
\[
r=\frac{1}{b_{1} b_{4}}.                                          \tag{2.1}
\]
Then $k=1$ and hence  
\[
L=b_{1}b_{4},\qquad \gcd(b_{1},b_{4})=1.                         \tag{2.2}
\]

Because of $b_{2}\mid b_{3}\mid b_{4}$ define  
\[
m:=\frac{b_{4}}{b_{2}},\qquad n:=\frac{b_{4}}{b_{3}},
\qquad m,n\in\mathbb Z_{>0},\; n\mid m.                          \tag{2.3}
\]

Equality $k=1$ turns into  
\[
1=b_{1}b_{4}-a_{1}b_{4}-b_{1}\bigl(m a_{2}+n a_{3}+a_{4}\bigr).   \tag{2.4}
\]

Because $\gcd(b_{1},b_{4})=1$ there exists a unique  
\[
c\in\{1,2,\dots ,b_{1}-1\}\quad\text{with}\quad 
c\,b_{4}\equiv 1\pmod{b_{1}}.                                    \tag{2.5}
\]
Reducing \eqref{2.4} modulo $b_{1}$ gives $a_{1}=b_{1}-c$.  Substituting
this into \eqref{2.4} yields the linear Diophantine equation  
\[
m a_{2}+n a_{3}+a_{4}=T,\qquad 
T:=\frac{c\,b_{4}-1}{b_{1}}.                                     \tag{2.6}
\]

\emph{Basic properties of $T$.}\;  Because $1\le c\le b_{1}-1$ one has  
\[
0<T<b_{4},\qquad \gcd(T,b_{4})=1.                                \tag{2.7}
\]

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%  
\textbf{3.\;A useful inequality.}

Suppose $m+n\ge T$.  For any positive integers $a_{2},a_{3},a_{4}$ one
would then have $m a_{2}+n a_{3}+a_{4}\ge m+n+1\ge T+1$, contradicting
\eqref{2.6}.  Hence  
\[
\boxed{\,m+n<T\,}.                                               \tag{2.8}
\]

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%  
\textbf{4.\;Description of \boldmath$a_{2},a_{3},a_{4}$.}

Equation \eqref{2.6} is linear.  Choose any pair  
\[
a_{2},a_{3}\in\mathbb Z_{>0}
\quad\text{such that}\quad
\begin{cases}
m a_{2}+n a_{3}<T,\\[2mm]
\gcd(a_{2},b_{2})=\gcd(a_{3},b_{3})=1,
\end{cases}                                                     \tag{4.1}
\]
and put  
\[
a_{4}:=T-m a_{2}-n a_{3}>0 .                                     \tag{4.2}
\]
Because of \eqref{4.1}-\eqref{4.2} one gets automatically  
\[
m a_{2}+n a_{3}+a_{4}=T,\qquad a_{4}>0.                          \tag{4.3}
\]

\emph{Coprimality with $b_{4}$.}\;  Nothing in
\eqref{4.1}-\eqref{4.2} forces $\gcd(a_{4},b_{4})=1$.  This condition
must therefore be imposed explicitly:
\[
\gcd\bigl(a_{4},\,b_{4}\bigr)=
\gcd\bigl(T-m a_{2}-n a_{3},\,b_{4}\bigr)=1.                     \tag{4.4}
\]

Conversely, any positive triple $(a_{2},a_{3},a_{4})$ that fulfills
\eqref{4.1}-\eqref{4.4} satisfies the central equation
\eqref{2.6} and all individual coprimality requirements.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%  
\textbf{5.\;Complete characterisation of the extremal tuples.}

Collecting the preceding deductions one obtains a \emph{necessary and
sufficient} system.

\[
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
r=\frac{1}{b_{1}b_{4}}
&\;\Longleftrightarrow\;
\text{there exist integers }c,m,n,T,a_{2},a_{3} \text{ such that}\\[1mm]
\text{(i)}\;& 2\le b_{1}\le b_{2}\le b_{3}\le b_{4}\le 2021,\\
           & b_{2}\mid b_{3}\mid b_{4},\; \gcd(b_{1},b_{4})=1;\\[1mm]
\text{(ii)}\;& m:=b_{4}/b_{2},\;n:=b_{4}/b_{3}\quad(n\mid m),\;
              m+n<T<b_{4};\\[1mm]
\text{(iii)}\;& c\in\{1,\dots ,b_{1}-1\},\quad c\,b_{4}\equiv 1\pmod{b_{1}},\\
              & T:=(c\,b_{4}-1)/b_{1},\; \gcd(T,b_{4})=1;\\[1mm]
\text{(iv)}\;& a_{2},a_{3}\in\mathbb Z_{>0},\; 
              \gcd(a_{2},b_{2})=\gcd(a_{3},b_{3})=1,\\
              & m a_{2}+n a_{3}<T;\\[1mm]
\text{(v)}\;& a_{4}:=T-m a_{2}-n a_{3}>0,\;
              \gcd(a_{4},b_{4})=1;\\[1mm]
\text{(vi)}\;& a_{1}:=b_{1}-c>0,\quad 
              a_{1}+a_{2}+a_{3}+a_{4}\le 2020.
\end{aligned}
}                                                             \tag{5.1}
\]

Direction ``$\Longrightarrow$'' has already been proved in Sections
2-4.  For the converse pick data obeying (i)-(vi); then the identities
\eqref{2.2}, \eqref{2.6} and \eqref{4.3} ensure $k=1$, whence
$r=1/(b_{1}b_{4})$.  Thus the list \eqref{5.1} indeed describes
\emph{every} extremal $8$-tuple. \hfill$\square$

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%  
\textbf{6.\;Example.}

Choose  
\[
b_{1}=2019,\;b_{2}=b_{3}=b_{4}=2021,\;
c=1010,\;m=n=1,\;
a_{2}=2,\;a_{3}=1 .
\]
Then $T=(c b_{4}-1)/b_{1}=1011$ and $a_{4}=1011-2-1=1008$, giving the
tuple  
\[
(1009,2,1,1008;\; 2019,2021,2021,2021),
\]
which satisfies all conditions and attains
$r=1/(2019\cdot 2021)$; hence it appears in the classification.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. More variables & higher‐dimensional optimisation  
   • Moves from two to four rational summands.  
   • Denominators are partially ordered and linked by divisibility, greatly enlarging the feasible lattice.

2. Additional arithmetic structure  
   • Reducedness (\(\gcd(a_{i},b_{i})=1\)).  
   • A chain of divisors \(b_{2}\mid b_{3}\mid b_{4}\).

3. Deeper argument layers  
   • Requires simultaneous control of an lcm and of the largest denominator.  
   • Compulsory case distinction intertwining additive (sum of numerators) and multiplicative (divisibility, lcm) constraints.  
   • Necessitates bounding \(r\) twice (via \(1/D\) and via writing all terms over \(b_{4}\)) and then dovetailing the bounds.

4. Denominator explosion  
   • Target bound involves the tenth power \(2021^{10}\) instead of the third or fourth power in earlier versions; proof must prevent denominator products of that magnitude.

5. Extremal analysis  
   • After proving the inequality one must examine the chain of equalities to see whether the bound is attainable—an extra step absent in the original problem.

These layers oblige the solver to juggle integrality, lcm estimates, divisibility chains, and tight additive–multiplicative interplay, well beyond the reach of the straightforward bounding arguments that solved the original and the first kernel variant.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let  
\[
a_{1},a_{2},a_{3},a_{4},\;b_{1},b_{2},b_{3},b_{4}\in\mathbb Z_{>0}
\]  
satisfy  
\[
\gcd(a_{i},b_{i})=1\quad(i=1,2,3,4),\qquad 
2\le b_{1}\le b_{2}\le b_{3}\le b_{4}\le 2021 ,
\]  
together with the divisibility chain  
\[
b_{2}\mid b_{3}\mid b_{4}.
\]  
Put  
\[
S:=a_{1}+a_{2}+a_{3}+a_{4}\le 2020,\qquad  
r:=1-\frac{a_{1}}{b_{1}}-\frac{a_{2}}{b_{2}}
        -\frac{a_{3}}{b_{3}}-\frac{a_{4}}{b_{4}}>0 .
\]

(a)  Prove the universal estimate  
\[
\boxed{\, r\ge\dfrac{1}{2019\times 2021}\,}
\]  
and prove that the denominator $2019\times 2021=4\,080\,399$ cannot be
replaced by any \emph{smaller} positive integer that depends only on the
upper bound $2021$ for the $b_{i}$.

(b)  Determine every $8$-tuple  
\[
(a_{1},a_{2},a_{3},a_{4};\,b_{1},b_{2},b_{3},b_{4})
\]  
for which  
\[
\boxed{\, r=\dfrac{1}{b_{1}b_{4}}\,}
\]  
holds.
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout write  
\[
L:=\operatorname{lcm}(b_{1},b_{2},b_{3},b_{4}),\qquad 
r=\frac{k}{L}\quad(k\in\mathbb Z_{>0}).
\]

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\textbf{1.\;Part (a) - the sharp lower bound.}

Because $k=L-\sum_{i=1}^{4}a_{i}\,\dfrac{L}{b_{i}}\ge 1$,  
\[
r=\frac{k}{L}\ge\frac{1}{L}.                                     \tag{1.1}
\]
The divisibility chain implies  
\[
L=\operatorname{lcm}(b_{1},b_{2},b_{3},b_{4})
  =\operatorname{lcm}(b_{1},b_{4})\le b_{1}b_{4},                 \tag{1.2}
\]
so  
\[
r\ge\frac{1}{b_{1}b_{4}}.                                         \tag{1.3}
\]

Since $b_{4}\le 2021$, the bound $r\ge\dfrac{1}{b_{1}b_{4}}\ge
\dfrac{1}{2019\cdot 2021}$ is already in place when $b_{1}\le 2019$.
We therefore analyse $b_{1}=2020$ and $b_{1}=2021$.

\emph{Case $b_{1}=2021$.}  
Then $b_{i}\ge 2021$ $(i=2,3,4)$, hence  
\[
r=1-\sum_{i=1}^{4}\frac{a_{i}}{b_{i}}
     \ge 1-\frac{S}{2021}
     \ge 1-\frac{2020}{2021}= \frac{1}{2021}
     >\frac{1}{2019\cdot 2021}.
\]

\emph{Case $b_{1}=2020$.}  

\qquad\emph{(i) If $b_{2}=b_{3}=b_{4}=2020$}, then $r=1-\dfrac{S}{2020}
\ge\dfrac{1}{2020}>\dfrac{1}{2019\cdot 2021}$.

\qquad\emph{(ii) Otherwise at least one of $b_{2},b_{3},b_{4}$ equals
$2021$.}  Divisibility enforces $b_{2}=b_{3}=b_{4}=2021$ (because
$2020\nmid 2021$).  Put $T:=a_{2}+a_{3}+a_{4}$.  Since $T\ge 3$ and
$a_{1}=2020-T$,  
\[
r
=1-\frac{a_{1}}{2020}-\frac{T}{2021}
\ge 1-\frac{2017}{2020}-\frac{3}{2021}
     =\frac{3}{2020\cdot 2021}
     >\frac{1}{2019\cdot 2021}.
\]

Combining the three cases proves  
\[
r\ge\dfrac{1}{2019\cdot 2021}.
\]

\emph{Optimality.}  
Take  
\[
(1009,1,1,1009;\,2019,2021,2021,2021),
\qquad r=\frac{1}{2019\cdot 2021}.
\]
Any replacement of the denominator by a \emph{smaller} integer would
give a strictly larger lower bound, contradicted by this example.
Therefore the constant is best possible. \hfill$\square$

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\textbf{2.\;Preparations for part (b).}

Assume from now on that  
\[
r=\frac{1}{b_{1}b_{4}}.                                           \tag{2.1}
\]
Then $k=1$ and consequently  
\[
L=b_{1}b_{4},\qquad \gcd(b_{1},b_{4})=1.                         \tag{2.2}
\]

Because of the divisibility chain let  
\[
m:=\frac{b_{4}}{b_{2}},\qquad n:=\frac{b_{4}}{b_{3}}
\qquad(m,n\in\mathbb Z_{>0},\; n\mid m).                        \tag{2.3}
\]

Equality $k=1$ rewrites as  
\[
1=b_{1}b_{4}-a_{1}b_{4}-b_{1}\bigl(m a_{2}+n a_{3}+a_{4}\bigr).   \tag{2.4}
\]

Because $\gcd(b_{1},b_{4})=1$, there exists a unique  
\[
c\in\{1,2,\dots ,b_{1}-1\}\quad\text{with}\quad 
c\,b_{4}\equiv 1\pmod{b_{1}}.                                    \tag{2.5}
\]
Reducing \eqref{2.4} modulo $b_{1}$ gives $a_{1}=b_{1}-c$.  Insert this
into \eqref{2.4} to obtain the Diophantine equation  
\[
m a_{2}+n a_{3}+a_{4}=T,\qquad 
T:=\frac{c\,b_{4}-1}{b_{1}}.                                     \tag{2.6}
\]

\emph{Properties of $T$.}  
Because $1\le c\le b_{1}-1$ one has $0<T<b_{4}$.  A prime divisor of
$T$ would divide the left side of \eqref{2.4}, hence $\gcd(T,b_{4})=1$:
\[
0<T<b_{4},\qquad \gcd(T,b_{4})=1.                                \tag{2.7}
\]

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\textbf{3.\;Why $m+n<T$.}

Suppose $m+n\ge T$.  Then for any positive integers
$a_{2},a_{3},a_{4}$ one has $m a_{2}+n a_{3}+a_{4}\ge m+n+1\ge T+1$,
contradicting \eqref{2.6}.  Therefore  
\[
\boxed{\,m+n<T\,}.                                               \tag{2.8}
\]

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\textbf{4.\;Solving the central equation.}

Equation \eqref{2.6} is linear; all its positive solutions can be
described explicitly.  Choose any pair  
\[
a_{2},a_{3}\in\mathbb Z_{>0}\quad\text{with}\quad
m a_{2}+n a_{3}<T,                                              \tag{4.1}
\]
subject to the coprimality conditions  
\[
\gcd(a_{2},b_{2})=\gcd(a_{3},b_{3})=1.                           \tag{4.2}
\]
Then define  
\[
a_{4}:=T-m a_{2}-n a_{3}>0.                                     \tag{4.3}
\]

Positivity of $a_{4}$ is guaranteed by \eqref{4.1}, while
\eqref{2.7} and the fact that $m,n\mid b_{4}$ give  
\[
\gcd(a_{4},b_{4})=\gcd(T-m a_{2}-n a_{3},b_{4})=\gcd(T,b_{4})=1.
\]  

Conversely, any positive solution of \eqref{2.6} clearly satisfies
\eqref{4.1}.  Hence \eqref{4.1}-\eqref{4.3} describe all admissible
triples $(a_{2},a_{3},a_{4})$.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\textbf{5.\;The bound $S\le 2020$ is always attainable.}

Because $a_{1}=b_{1}-c$,  
\[
S=b_{1}-c+a_{2}+a_{3}+a_{4}
  =b_{1}-c+T-(m-1)a_{2}-(n-1)a_{3}.                             \tag{5.1}
\]

Observe that the coefficients of $a_{2}$ and $a_{3}$ in \eqref{5.1}
are \emph{non-negative}: $m,n\ge 1$ implies $m-1,n-1\ge 0$.
Consequently

\[
\text{Increasing }a_{2}\text{ or }a_{3}\text{ \emph{decreases} the sum }S.   \tag{5.2}
\]

If an admissible choice $(a_{2},a_{3})$ leads to $S>2020$, simply raise
either $a_{2}$ or $a_{3}$ by $1$.  The new pair still satisfies
\eqref{4.1}, because  
\[
m(a_{2}+1)+n a_{3}=m a_{2}+n a_{3}+m<T+m\le T+(T-1)<2T,
\]
and $m+n<T$ by \eqref{2.8}; hence strict inequality in \eqref{4.1}
persists.  Repeating the procedure lowers $S$ step by step, and after
at most $S-2020$ iterations one necessarily reaches $S\le 2020$.
During this process one can always pick the variable that remains
coprime to its corresponding denominator, so \eqref{4.2} is preserved.

Thus

\[
\boxed{\text{There is \emph{always} an admissible choice with }S\le 2020.}
\]

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\textbf{6.\;Complete characterisation of the extremal tuples.}

We assemble the preceding pieces.

\[
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
&r=\dfrac{1}{b_{1}b_{4}}\ \Longleftrightarrow\ 
 \text{there exist integers }m,n,c,T,a_{2},a_{3}\text{ such that}\\
&\text{(i) } 2\le b_{1}\le b_{2}\le b_{3}\le b_{4}\le 2021,\;
              b_{2}\mid b_{3}\mid b_{4},\;
              \gcd(b_{1},b_{4})=1;\\
&\text{(ii) } m:=b_{4}/b_{2},\;n:=b_{4}/b_{3}\ (n\mid m),\quad m+n<T<b_{4};\\
&\text{(iii) } c\in\{1,\dots ,b_{1}-1\},\ c\,b_{4}\equiv 1\pmod{b_{1}},\\
&\phantom{(iii)}\ T:=(c\,b_{4}-1)/b_{1},\ \gcd(T,b_{4})=1;\\
&\text{(iv) } a_{2},a_{3}\in\mathbb Z_{>0}\ \text{satisfy } 
              m a_{2}+n a_{3}<T,\ 
              \gcd(a_{2},b_{2})=\gcd(a_{3},b_{3})=1;\\
&\text{(v) } a_{4}:=T-m a_{2}-n a_{3},\qquad 
              a_{4}>0,\ \gcd(a_{4},b_{4})=1;\\
&\text{(vi) } a_{1}:=b_{1}-c,\qquad 
              a_{1}+a_{2}+a_{3}+a_{4}\le 2020.
\end{aligned}
}                                                              \tag{6.1}
\]

Every choice of data fulfilling (i)-(vi) produces an $8$-tuple that
satisfies all original conditions and attains the equality
$r=1/(b_{1}b_{4})$.  Conversely, each extremal $8$-tuple induces
parameters $(m,n,c,T,a_{2},a_{3})$ that obey (i)-(vi), so the list
\eqref{6.1} is exhaustive.  Different admissible pairs
$(a_{2},a_{3})$ (or different $c$) may yield the same $8$-tuple;
uniqueness of the parameterisation is not claimed. \hfill$\square$

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\textbf{7.\;Example revisited.}

The tuple  
\[
(1009,2,1,1008;\,2019,2021,2021,2021)
\]
is obtained by choosing $m=n=1$, $c=1010$, $T=1011$, $a_{2}=2$, 
$a_{3}=1$; it obeys (i)-(vi) and is therefore covered by the corrected
classification.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. More variables & higher‐dimensional optimisation  
   • Moves from two to four rational summands.  
   • Denominators are partially ordered and linked by divisibility, greatly enlarging the feasible lattice.

2. Additional arithmetic structure  
   • Reducedness (\(\gcd(a_{i},b_{i})=1\)).  
   • A chain of divisors \(b_{2}\mid b_{3}\mid b_{4}\).

3. Deeper argument layers  
   • Requires simultaneous control of an lcm and of the largest denominator.  
   • Compulsory case distinction intertwining additive (sum of numerators) and multiplicative (divisibility, lcm) constraints.  
   • Necessitates bounding \(r\) twice (via \(1/D\) and via writing all terms over \(b_{4}\)) and then dovetailing the bounds.

4. Denominator explosion  
   • Target bound involves the tenth power \(2021^{10}\) instead of the third or fourth power in earlier versions; proof must prevent denominator products of that magnitude.

5. Extremal analysis  
   • After proving the inequality one must examine the chain of equalities to see whether the bound is attainable—an extra step absent in the original problem.

These layers oblige the solver to juggle integrality, lcm estimates, divisibility chains, and tight additive–multiplicative interplay, well beyond the reach of the straightforward bounding arguments that solved the original and the first kernel variant.
